point_001: It is unclear whether the model is genuinely learning to follow instructions or simply memorizing a large space of tasks during fine-tuning; no experiments rule out the possibility that nonsense or absent prompts would yield similar performance.
point_002: The ablation on the number of instruction-tuning clusters (Section 4.1) is inconclusive: most gains could stem from the hardest or most similar tasks rather than from broader task coverage, and the analysis cannot distinguish these explanations.
point_003: The paper lacks important practical details such as hardware specifications and total training time.
point_004: The role of the instructions themselves is not clearly established; it is not demonstrated that the natural-language instruction format (as opposed to arbitrary or non-instructional templates) is what drives performance improvements on held-out tasks.
point_005: The explanation for why instruction tuning only benefits sufficiently large models (≥68B parameters) is speculative; an alternative explanation—that larger pre-trained models have better out-of-distribution generalization rather than simply more capacity—is not empirically tested.
point_006: The reason why the untuned base model's zero-shot performance degrades with increasing model size (Figure 6A) is unexplained and appears to contradict prior findings (e.g., Brown et al.) that larger models consistently improve on most tasks.
point_007: The comparison between FLAN and GPT-3 is not fully fair because the two models differ in both size and pre-training data distribution; Base LM vs. FLAN would be a more controlled comparison for isolating the effect of instruction tuning.
point_008: Figure 1 may be misleading because zero-shot FLAN underperforms few-shot GPT-3 on the majority of individual tasks, yet the figure could give the opposite impression.
point_009: The significant performance drop on ReCorD after instruction tuning (noted in Appendix A.1) is not discussed or explained.
point_010: The analysis in Appendix C (prompt/prefix tuning on top of FLAN) covers only classification tasks; extending it to generation tasks such as summarization or question answering, and including a baseline of directly fine-tuning Base LM without prompts, would strengthen the conclusions.
point_011: It is not investigated whether using all available training examples per dataset is optimal, since the model could overfit to dataset-specific spurious correlations; the effect of training data size per dataset is unexplored.
point_012: The justification and mechanics of the OPTIONS token are insufficiently explained, including whether fine-tuned models concentrate probability mass on the provided options and how the Base LM (which is not fine-tuned) handles this token.
point_013: The paper does not explore applying FLAN-style instruction prompts to GPT-3 or Base LM directly, which would help isolate whether the prompt format alone (without fine-tuning) accounts for some of the observed gains.
point_014: The novelty of the method is limited, as it is a straightforward scaling-up of concepts from prior works on multi-task prompted fine-tuning (e.g., Zhong et al., Mishra et al.), and the paper does not sufficiently differentiate its contribution from these predecessors.
